MOTION For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Nov-15-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Raven Sarnoff, Esq. 650-393-4995 of Sarnoff & Sarnoff for Plaintiff.
Linda Ross, Esq. 415-517-0851 of Renne Public Law for Defendant SAM ROMERO.
Jenica Maldonado, Deputy City Attorney, 415-554-3915 for Defendant CCSF.

RULING - A R G U E D. Counsel for Defendant Romero argues, 3 elements have not been satisfied. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT SAM ROMERO's MOTION For Judgment On The Pleadings.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Sam Romero's motion for judgment on the pleadings on the sole cause of action for harassment based on sex and/or gender in violation of FEHA alleged against him in the complaint is denied. The court did not consider the complaint filed by Ms. Montes in federal court or the misdemeanor complaint filed in San Mateo Superior Court. Neither Miller v. Department of Corrections (2005) 36 Cal. 4th 446 nor Proksel v. Gattis (1996) 41 Cal. App. 4th 1626 requires an allegation of more than a single consensual relationship to support an actionable paramour harassment claim. (Negrete v. Meadowbrook Meat Co. (C.D. Cal. 2012) 2012 WL 254039, *7 ("Miller expressly addressed Proksel and noted that it did not announce a per se rule that a plaintiff could not state a claim 'based upon favoritism arising from a single affair in a small office.'")). Proksel, 41 Cal. App. 4th at 1630-31 took "pains" to emphasize that the plaintiff in that case would have had a viable claim based on a single consensual relationship had she presented additional facts beyond favoritism to the paramour that, among other things, showed the existence of a hostile work environment. Miller, 36 Cal. 4th at 466 held that "an employee may establish an actionable claim of sexual harassment under FEHA by demonstrating that widespread sexual favoritism was severe or pervasive enough to alter his or her working conditions and create a hostile work environment." The determination of whether there is actionable "widespread sexual favoritism" as distinguished from non-actionable "isolated favoritism" depends not on the extent or number of paramour relationships, but the "effect" of those relationships on the workplace. (Id. at 463-64, 471-72). Plaintiffs have alleged sufficient ultimate facts showing that the alleged hostile effects of the alleged Romero-Montes relationship on them and the Station 2 workplace was severe, pervasive, widespread and resulted in significantly differential treatment of Station 2 employees based on gender. Most notably, and an allegation that Mr. Romero fails to adequately address in his memoranda, plaintiffs allege based on sufficient ultimate facts that among the many negative effects of the Romero-Montero relationship was the endangerment of the safety of those who worked at Station 2 and the public because Ms. Montes was permitted to avoid necessary training and was given preferential assignments which otherwise would have been given to firefighters who had greater familiarity with the equipment involved in the assignments.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/HK)
